text.title: Card Sorting Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every Card Sorting application MUST enforce, plus a skip-this-methodology fallback. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: Open sort MUST have ≥15 participants; closed sort MUST have ≥30. Lower counts are rejected.
rule.rationale: Below threshold the similarity matrix is too noisy to identify confident groupings.

rule.statement: Cards MUST be written in user language (the words users use), not the team's internal product taxonomy.
rule.rationale: Internal-language cards bias the sort toward the existing taxonomy and defeat the purpose.

rule.statement: Analysis MUST include a similarity matrix and an agreement score per grouping. Eyeballed groupings without quantification are rejected.
rule.rationale: Eyeballed groupings reflect researcher mental models, not participant ones.

rule.statement: Items with <60% agreement MUST be flagged for re-test, not silently assigned.
rule.rationale: Forcing weak-agreement items into a category degrades the IA in a way that re-testing would reveal.

rule.statement: The artefact MUST emit a recommended structure (category names + item membership) with one-sentence rationale per category. Result-only dumps without recommendation are rejected.
rule.rationale: The recommendation is the actionable output; the matrix is the evidence.

rule.statement: If any Applies-If precondition fails OR any Skip-If condition triggers, do NOT apply this methodology. Emit a skip-record naming the failed precondition / triggered skip, and route the task to the correct methodology (or none).
rule.rationale: Methodology hygiene: mis-application produces worse artefacts than no methodology because downstream consumers trust the schema; the skip-record makes non-application traceable.
